outlook.headline: Memory supply stays tight as capacity additions lag demand
outlook.summary: Memory supply looks set to remain constrained through 2026Q1–Q2: TSMC and Micron point to severe undersupply and node/packaging limits, while Avnet and Arrow are still describing thin channel availability. That supports pricing and utilization for suppliers, but keeps pressure on PC/mobile/handset-exposed names; the latest mention trend is still elevated at 0.6x, 14% above trailing average, so tightness is still very much in focus. Watch whether new capacity ramps start to ease the bottleneck, or whether allocation persists longer than expected.

drivers.label: AI-led undersupply and bit-growth constraints
drivers.detail: Severe undersupply from AI demand plus cleanroom, node and packaging limits are keeping supply below demand.

drivers.label: Channel inventories remain thin
drivers.detail: Avnet is adding memory inventory to support customer programs and Arrow says on-prem supply is becoming thin, both consistent with allocation-like conditions.

drivers.label: Multiple bottlenecks still visible across the chain
drivers.detail: Tester shortages, DRAM capacity constraints, and locked-up supply all point to continued scarcity rather than normalization.

risks: New DRAM/NAND capacity and packaging ramps could ease shortages faster than expected.
Shortage-driven demand destruction in PCs, mobile and handsets could soften downstream pull.
Customer inventory rebuilds could fade if programs slow, reducing the need for buffering.
